narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication with your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.80, which stands out well above the typical student's average of 3.21, and your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. Your family comes from a lower-middle income background, and while your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both sit at the 1st level—below the averages of around the 5th or 6th deciles—your LSAT score of 22 is notably lower than the usual 37. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 49% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test, tipping the scale just enough to reject your application. This decision hinged most on your undergraduate GPA, which strongly pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass, acting as your biggest asset. However, your 3rd-year school decile ranking worked against you, pushing the model more toward anticipating a failure more than any other factor. Your LSAT score and 1st-year decile also nudged things slightly in the unfavorable direction, though less forcefully, while your family income and full-time status offered minor boosts toward success. Compared to typical students, your stellar GPA gives you an edge many lack, but the lower LSAT and early-year deciles align you closer to profiles the model sees as riskier for the bar. It's tough to hear this, especially with your strengths shining through, but it reflects how these academic markers weighed against you in this predictive lens. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score toward the average, seeking ways to improve your school rankings through targeted study or programs, and highlighting your unique resilience as a Black woman in applications to schools with holistic reviews. You have real potential—many overcome similar hurdles—and exploring bar prep resources early could build your confidence for the long game. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
